title: Finding the Peak
concept: At the peak or valley of any curve, the slope (derivative) becomes exactly zero, showing we've reached a maximum or minimum point.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a derivative measure?

options.text: The rate of change of an output relative to a tiny change in input
The total sum of all outputs in the dataset
The average value of the inputs

exercises.explain: Yes! A derivative measures the rate of change. But what happens to this rate when we reach the very top of a hill?

cards.title: Reaching the summit
cards.body: As you climb a hill, the ground is steep. But at the exact peak, the ground beneath your feet becomes perfectly flat for a brief moment.

cards.title: Zero slope at the top
cards.body: In calculus, when the derivative (slope) of a curve is exactly zero, you have reached a stationary point — either a peak (maximum) or a valley (minimum).

cards.title: A marble in a bowl
cards.body: Think of rolling a marble in a bowl. It rolls down the steep sides where the slope is high, and comes to rest at the very bottom where the ground is flat (slope = 0).

cards.title: Peak profit
cards.body: If you model a business's profit based on price, the peak profit is where the derivative of profit with respect to price is zero. Charging more or less from there reduces profit.

cards.title: Not out of data
cards.body: A slope of zero doesn't mean you've run out of data. It just means the curve has leveled out, indicating you've hit a peak, a valley, or a flat plateau.

left.text:
Slope is zero at the top
Nudging input goes down

right.text:
Slope is zero at the bottom
Nudging input goes up

exercises.prompt: What is the slope (derivative) of a curve at its highest or lowest point?
exercises.explain: At the peak or valley, the curve flattens out, meaning the slope is exactly zero.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A slope of zero always means you have reached the worst possible error in a model.
exercises.explain: False! A slope of zero could mean a valley (the lowest possible error) or a peak.

exercises.sentence: To find the peak of a profit curve, we look for the point where the derivative is ___.
exercises.explain: Peak points occur where the slope flattens out to zero.

exercises.prompt: Which graph has reached a peak or valley at the marked point?

options.text: Graph A: The line is perfectly flat at its lowest point
Graph B: The line is climbing steeply upward
Graph C: The line is plunging steeply downward

exercises.explain: A flat line at the bottom indicates a valley (minimum) where the slope is zero.

exercises.prompt: An advertising model tracks how sales respond to ad spend. The derivative of sales with respect to ad spend is currently positive. What should you do to reach peak sales?

options.text: Increase ad spend, because a positive slope means sales are still climbing
Decrease ad spend, because you have already passed the peak
Keep ad spend exactly the same, because you are at the peak

exercises.explain: Excellent! A positive derivative means you are still climbing the hill, so you should keep going until the slope hits zero. You've mastered how to locate peaks and valleys! Next up: handling multiple knobs at once!
